And this is something that we all understand.
But something that I think is overlooked is that inference.
So once you have a train model now deploying it and making predictions for users is arguably even more expensive.
Like if you look at the lifetime of a model, it probably exceeds the training costs, according to this analysis within just one week of use.
And so if your model is being used for months or years or many people, the cost will like greatly eclipse the training costs, which is more of a one time cost.
And this is a problem because if we want to make AI systems able to help lots of different people in different places, people without as much resources or access power.
We want to be able to reduce the cost of serving AI systems to the public.
And this is also getting harder because models are getting bigger.
And like maybe a slight shift towards reducing model size a little bit in the last maybe two years, but these models are still like billions of parameters in size and that is expensive to serve.
So the main question of this that we'll be talking about in today's lecture is how can we cheaply, efficiently and equitably deploy NLP systems without sacrificing performance.

There's like a clear answer here that I'm kind of leading towards, which is model compression and model compression here basically means taking a trained model and then reducing the size of that model before deploying it.
And there's three high level ways that we'll talk about in today's lecture for how we can compress models.

The first is quantization, which is you basically don't really change the architecture parameters of the model up to a certain amount of precision and then you throw away the remainder of the position.
And the second is pruning so throwing out entire components or parameters of a model.

And the third is distillation where you might change all of the parameters, but you're basically condensing the knowledge of a big model into a smaller model that is retrained often from scratch to replicate the behavior of the big model.
So I'll go into much more detail with all these things.

The basic meat the meaning of this term is that you have a model that has usually more parameters than you have training data or more casual terms you just have a lot of parameters like way more than statistical machine learning would say you need.
So for example, a GPT three the original GPT three model had 170 billion parameters, which is like definitely over parameterize.
And so there's been a lot of work in the theory community of ML that shows that over parameterized models models that have a huge number parameters are actually much easier to train.

And so that's not especially for very complicated tasks and the basic idea is that training deep neural networks for for most tasks requires optimizing a non convex objective, which is not guaranteed to you're not guaranteed to find the global optimum of a non convex objective.

And then you just reduce the precision of say all of the weights in that model.

But if you instead reduce the precision of the parameters of the weights in that model, you see like a pretty massive decrease, which is linear to the reduction precision.
At the most extreme case, if you replaced each float 32 with a single bit so zero or one.
Then you would only have like an eight gigabyte model, which you could probably load into most GPUs.
